ocr.text: Fitness Costs Associated with A. glycines Biotypes
Fig 3. Addition of an inducer population affects subsequent fitness of virulent biotypes on a
susceptible cultivar. This effect was observed by varying inducer population biotypes on varying response
population biotypes on susceptible soybean. For this experiment the susceptible cultivar IA3027, Rag1
cultivar IA3027RA1, and Rag2 cultivar IA3027RA2 were used. Capital letters indicate significance among
Modeling the consequences of virulent biotype fitness costs and induced
In general, fitness costs and negative cross-resistance delayed the development of virulence.
This delay was observed when virulence was rare (i.e., 0.02), regardless of the mode of inheri-
tance. When virulence was common (i.e., 0.2), the delay was only observed with recessive or
additive modes of inheritance (Table 1). Fitness costs resulted in the frequency of additively
